turns.content: happy new year. okay so i've been thinking about this a lot over the holidays — do you think formal design education is still worth it in 2025? like a four year degree specifically. a younger cousin asked me for advice and i honestly don't know what to tell her

Happy new year! That's a big question, and the honest answer is "it depends" — which I know is frustrating.

The case **for** a design degree:
- Structured foundation in design thinking, theory, and critique
- Access to mentors and peers who push your work
- Portfolio and career infrastructure (internships, alumni networks)
- Forces you through uncomfortable projects that broaden your range
- Some employers and agencies still filter for degrees

The case **against**:
- Tools and technical skills can be self-taught (YouTube, online courses, practice)
- Four years is a long time, and the industry moves fast
- Tuition cost can be staggering depending on the program
- Portfolio matters far more than credentials when actually hiring

The real question for your cousin is: what kind of designer does she want to be? A degree matters more for some paths (brand strategy, design leadership, academia) than others (freelance UI, web design, social media graphics).

Did you go the formal education route yourself?

yeah i did. i went to tama art university in tokyo for my BFA. honestly it was a completely different world from what i'd been doing on my own before that. like i thought i was pretty decent at design going in and then the first semester absolutely humbled me

Tama Art University is a seriously strong program — one of the top art schools in Japan. The humbling first semester is almost a universal experience at schools of that caliber. You arrive thinking you know things, and then you're surrounded by people who challenge everything you thought was good.

What specifically shifted for you there? Was it more about technical skill development, or conceptual thinking, or something else entirely? That might help clarify what advice to give your cousin.

honestly the biggest thing was typography. before tama i thought typography was just picking a nice font and making it look good. their type program was on a completely different level — we studied letterform anatomy, kerning and spacing at the micro level, optical alignment versus mathematical alignment, type history all the way back to movable type. that's where i genuinely fell in love with type design as its own discipline. i still hear my typography professor's voice in my head almost daily when i'm working

That's a perfect example of something that's nearly impossible to get outside of a rigorous academic program. You can watch a YouTube tutorial on font pairing, but studying letterform anatomy, learning why optical alignment differs from mathematical alignment, understanding the historical arc from movable type through phototypesetting to digital — that depth of knowledge fundamentally changes how you see type.

And the fact that your professor's lessons are still echoing years later — that's the real value. Not the degree on the wall, but the foundational thinking it instilled.

For your cousin, this is worth framing as: **what do you want your foundation to be?**

- If she wants depth — the kind of understanding that makes you a better designer for decades — a strong program is worth it
- If she wants to start earning and building a portfolio quickly, she can self-teach tools and get client work within a year or so

Does she know what area of design she's interested in?

she's into illustration and character design mostly. she already does a lot of digital illustration and she's honestly pretty talented for her age. she's been looking at a few schools but the tuition is making her hesitate, which i totally get. i was lucky that tama had decent financial aid options but even with that it was a stretch for my family

Illustration and character design is an interesting case because the career paths are quite specific — gaming, animation, editorial, merchandise, publishing. And the value of a degree varies across those.

**Where a degree helps for illustration:**
- Animation studios and game companies often recruit directly through art school pipelines. The school connections genuinely matter
- Fundamentals like anatomy, color theory, composition, and life drawing are taught rigorously in a structured environment with critique — hard to replicate alone
- Portfolio reviews and industry-specific career guidance are built into good programs

**Where self-teaching can work:**
- Freelance editorial and merchandise illustration — clients care about your portfolio and style, not your diploma
- Building an online following (Twitter/X, Instagram, Pixiv) can generate work independently of credentials

For the tuition concern, a few practical angles:
- Some schools have strong scholarship or work-study programs — worth investigating specifically for each school
- If she's comparing schools, weigh the program's industry connections against the tuition. A more affordable school with strong studio partnerships might outperform an expensive one without them
- A two-year senmon gakkou focused on illustration might give her concentrated training at significantly lower cost, though the experience is different from a four-year university

What schools is she looking at?

she mentioned musashino and kyoto seika, plus a couple vocational schools in osaka. honestly both of those universities are really strong for illustration. i keep going back and forth on what to tell her because like... my tama experience was genuinely transformative for me but i also wonder if that's just survivorship bias? plenty of incredible designers never set foot in art school

That's a really honest self-check. Survivorship bias is a legitimate concern — you had a great experience at a great school, so naturally you'd advocate for it. But you're right that plenty of brilliant designers are entirely self-taught.

Here's how to cut through the bias:

**Ask yourself what you got from Tama that you genuinely could NOT have gotten elsewhere:**
- The typography immersion — could you have found that depth on your own? Probably not at the same level or pace
- The peer environment — being surrounded by talented, driven people who challenged your work daily
- The structured critique — having professors honestly dissect your work week after week
- The discipline of completing projects you wouldn't have chosen for yourself

**Now ask what you could have learned without it:**
- Figma, Illustrator, Photoshop — tool skills are completely self-teachable
- Client management, pricing, business of freelancing — school probably didn't teach you these at all
- Specific industry trends — these change too fast for any curriculum to keep up

For your cousin specifically: Musashino and Kyoto Seika both have excellent illustration programs with real industry connections. If she gets into either, the peer environment and faculty alone are worth serious consideration. The vocational schools will get her job-ready faster but with less conceptual depth.

Honest advice to give her: if she can afford it through aid or scholarships without crippling debt, the four-year university experience at a strong program compounds over an entire career. But only if the financial math doesn't break her.

yeah the "what could you NOT have gotten elsewhere" test is really clarifying. for me it's clearly the type program and the critique culture. like i remember being in the studio at 4am working on projects surrounded by other students doing the same thing and everyone feeding off each other's energy and giving each other feedback. you just can't replicate that energy watching tutorials in your bedroom

That 4am studio energy with your peers is exactly the thing that can't be downloaded or streamed. The shared intensity of it — seeing someone next to you solve a problem brilliantly while you're stuck, the spontaneous critiques at 2am, pushing each other because you're all in it together — that's the actual product of art school. Not the diploma.

And that collaborative pressure is especially relevant for illustration. Drawing is often a solitary practice, so having a cohort around you who sees your process, not just your finished work, accelerates growth in a way that posting to Instagram and getting likes never will.

Here's what I'd suggest telling your cousin:

1. **Visit the schools in person.** Walk through the studios, talk to current students, sit in on a critique if possible. If the energy feels right, that's a powerful signal.

2. **Look at the faculty's actual work.** At schools like Musashino and Kyoto Seika, the illustration professors are often practicing artists. Their portfolios tell you what kind of mentorship you'll get.

3. **Ask about financial aid immediately.** Don't let the sticker price be the only number she sees. Scholarships and aid can change the calculation completely.

4. **Remind her the decision isn't irreversible.** If she starts at a vocational school and wants more depth later, paths exist. If she starts at a university and wants to work sooner, she can freelance during school.

that's all really solid advice. i'm going to basically relay this whole framework to her. especially the visiting part — i remember the exact moment i walked into tama's studios for the first time during an open campus day and just knowing that was where i needed to be. that gut feeling ended up being completely right

The campus visit gut check is real — and for creative programs especially, the physical environment tells you everything. The quality of the studios, the work pinned up on the walls, whether students look engaged or just going through the motions. Your cousin will know when she walks through the right door.

One more thing to mention to her: she should bring her portfolio to those visits if she can. Talking to a professor or current student while showing her actual work will give her much more specific feedback than just browsing brochures. It also signals seriousness, which can matter for admissions later.

You clearly got a lot out of your time at Tama, and the fact that you're thoughtful enough to question your own bias before advising her speaks well of the advice she'll get. Not every cousin has a working designer to talk this through with.

honestly i hope i can be helpful to her. the whole thing is making me a bit nostalgic about my own school days lol. okay this has been really useful, i feel way more confident about what to say to her now. thanks for helping me think through it

A little nostalgia for the 4am studio days isn't the worst thing — it means the experience genuinely mattered to you, which is the clearest signal you can pass along.

To sum up for her:

- Strong programs are worth it for the foundation, critique culture, and peer environment — especially for illustration where drawing fundamentals compound over a career
- Visit the schools, feel the energy, talk to students and faculty
- Investigate financial aid before ruling anything out on cost alone
- The degree isn't the point — the transformation is

And if she ends up going the vocational route or self-teaching, that can absolutely work too. The path matters less than the dedication she brings to it. Either way, she's starting from a good place if she's already doing solid illustration work at her age.

Good luck with the conversation — and happy new year again.
